original_abstract: China has witnessed several peaceful and successful anti-PX (para-xylene) protests in Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo in re- cent years. However, the protest in Maoming in 2014 turned out to be a noteworthy exception. By using participant observation and in- depth interview data, this article raises the following observations: first, in spite of official propaganda, Maoming citizens actually un- derstand the environmental risk of PX production because of their personal experiences with existing pollution as well as from their knowledge of the Xiamen incident. Secondly, the Maoming protest proceeded in a less organised manner, which explains its violent tendencies. Finally, the large-scale protest was able to proceed without the support of mainstream media and the middle class due to the use of online social media and local knowledge of the urban terrain.
humanized_abstract: It has become routine to speak of a wave of unarmed and non-violent campaigns against the construction of petrochemical plants producing PX (para-xylene) in Xiamen, Dalian and Ningbo in the past few years. As far as I know, Maoming was the first exception in 2014. Through employing methods such as participant observation and in-depth interviews, this article will focus on three issues, namely that even when the official Chinese media promotes a new form of environmental governance which allegedly enhances citizens' awareness and ability to cope with environment hazards, environmental consciousness and civil resistance can emerge at the grass roots level whether it be because of past experiences with pollution in the same locality or at a distance or due to contagion effect from one campaign to another. The Maoming campaign was much less well organised than previous instances, a factor that possibly contributed to the degree of violence that was involved. In contrast with previous events that triggered extensive coverage from the mass media and drew upon the participation of the urban middle class, this large civil protest was based on social media and the local knowledge of the city and streets of Maoming.

However, the protest in Maoming in 2014 turned out to be a noteworthy exception. Secondly, the Maoming protest proceeded in a less organised manner, which explains its violent tendencies. Finally, the large-scale protest was able to proceed without the support of mainstream media and the middle class due to the use of online social media and local knowledge of the urban terrain.
